name: Hick's Law: Reduce and Structure Choices
trigger: A screen, menu, navigation, or form presents users with many simultaneous options or actions, or a decision point offers a long undifferentiated list.
recommendation: Reduce the number of simultaneous choices and structure those that remain: show the smallest set needed for the current task, group and categorise options, use progressive disclosure for advanced choices, highlight a recommended default, and break long decisions into sequential steps so each moment has fewer branches.
rejection: Reject dumping every possible action at one altitude, mega-menus with dozens of ungrouped links, settings pages that expose all options at once with no grouping, and pricing tables with so many tiers that comparison stalls.
validation: Count primary choices per decision point and challenge any with an unstructured long list
Verify options are grouped, ordered by likely use, or staged via disclosure
Confirm a sensible default or recommended option is offered where appropriate
evidence: Hick, W.E. (1952) and Hyman (1953): decision time increases logarithmically with the number of equally probable choices
Caveat: Hick's Law applies to undifferentiated simultaneous choices; well-grouped, scannable, or learned options do not incur the same cost
